title: Caramelised French Toast

ingredients.name: bread (thick slices, preferably day-old)
visual_checks: day-old milk bread or any white bread absorbs the custard without falling apart
ingredients.notes: day-old milk bread or any white bread absorbs the custard without falling apart

subs.name: thick-sliced brioche or milk pav for an even richer, softer result
3 tbsp thick curd thinned with 3 tbsp water — adds a subtle tang

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: 1 tbsp for the custard soak, 2 tbsp for caramelising on the pan
ingredients.notes: 1 tbsp for the custard soak, 2 tbsp for caramelising on the pan

subs.name: 2 tbsp jaggery powder for the caramelising step — gives a deeper, more complex caramel with notes of molasses
2 tbsp ghee for a nutty, aromatic alternative that caramelises beautifully

steps.text: Whisk together the eggs, milk, 1 tbsp sugar, vanilla extract, and cinnamon (if using) in a wide shallow bowl until smooth and uniform — this is the custard soak.

Lay the bread slices into the custard one at a time and let them soak for 30 seconds per side. The bread should be saturated but still holding together. Lift out with a slotted spatula, letting excess drip back into the bowl.

Heat 1 tbsp butter in a wide frying pan or tawa over medium flame. When the butter foams and the foam begins to subside, scatter 1 tbsp of sugar evenly over the pan base — it will begin to caramelise into a thin amber layer almost immediately.

Place 3 soaked bread slices into the pan in a single layer, pressing them gently into the caramel. Fry undisturbed for 2–3 minutes until the underside is deeply golden and the caramel is set and crisp. Flip carefully and cook the second side for 1–2 minutes until golden.
Transfer to a warm plate. Repeat with the remaining butter, sugar, and bread slices — each batch gets a fresh scatter of sugar.
Serve immediately, as the caramel crust softens within minutes. Top with a dusting of icing sugar, fresh fruit, honey, or simply eat them as they are while they are hot and crisp.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: bread → thick-sliced brioche or milk pav for an even richer, softer result; butter → 2 tbsp ghee for a nutty, aromatic alternative that caramelises beautifully; milk → 3 tbsp thick curd thinned with 3 tbsp water — adds a subtle tang; sugar → 2 tbsp jaggery powder for the caramelising step — gives a deeper, more complex caramel with notes of molasses
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A flat tawa works perfectly for this — the same setup used for making parathas. Use Amul butter or ghee; ghee gives a nuttier caramel that pairs especially well with jaggery. Day-old pav buns from the bakery, split open and soaked for the same time, are an excellent substitute for bread slices. Serve with a drizzle of honey or fresh banana slices for a quick, impressive breakfast.
